message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    To assess whether the basic reproduction number (
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    ) of COVID-19 is different across countries and what national-level demographic, social, and environmental factors characterize initial vulnerability to the virus.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We fit logistic growth curves to reported daily case numbers, up to the first epidemic peak. This fitting estimates
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    . We then use a generalized additive model to discern the effects, and include 5 random effect covariates to account for potential differences in testing and reporting that can bias the estimated
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We found that the mean R0 is 1.70 (S.D. 0.57), with a range between 1.10 (Ghana) and 3.52 (South Korea). We identified four factors-population between 20-34 years old (youth), population residing in urban agglomerates over 1 million (city), social media use to organize offline action (social media), and GINI income inequality-as having strong relationships with
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    . An intermediate level of youth and GINI inequality are associated with high
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    , while high city population and high social media use are associated with high
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    . Environmental and climate factors were not found to have strong relationships with
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Studies that aim to measure the effectiveness of interventions should account for the intrinsic differences between populations.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Social, economic, and environmental factors influencing the basic reproduction number of COVID-19 across countries
